content:
# Genesis Chapter 28 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 15v–16r | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_028_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/hebrew-OT/GEN_028_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Source:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), Pages 24–25 | **View LXX online:** https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209/0028

---

**1** Then Isaac called Jacob and blessed him and charged him, and said to him, "You shall not take a wife from the daughters of Canaan.

**2** Arise, go to Paddan-aram, to the house of Bethuel your mother's father, and take for yourself a wife from there, from the daughters of Laban your mother's brother.

**3** May God Almighty bless you and make you fruitful and multiply you, so that you may become a company of peoples.

**4** May he give to you the blessing of Abraham, to you and to your offspring with you, so that you may possess the land of your sojourning, which God gave to Abraham."

**5** So Isaac sent Jacob away, and he went to Paddan-aram, to Laban the son of Bethuel the Aramean, the brother of Rebekah, the mother of Jacob and Esau.

**6** Now Esau saw that Isaac had blessed Jacob and sent him away to Paddan-aram to take a wife from there, and that as he blessed him he charged him, saying, "You shall not take a wife from the daughters of Canaan,"

**7** and that Jacob had obeyed his father and his mother and had gone to Paddan-aram.

**8** So Esau saw that the daughters of Canaan were displeasing in the eyes of Isaac his father,

**9** and Esau went to Ishmael and took as his wife, in addition to the wives he had, Mahalath the daughter of Ishmael the son of Abraham, the sister of Nebaioth.

**10** Jacob went out from Beersheba and set out toward Haran.

**11** And he came upon a certain place and lodged there that night, because the sun had set. He took one of the stones of the place and put it under his head and lay down in that place to sleep.

**12** And he dreamed, and behold, there was a stairway set up on the earth, and the top of it reached to heaven. And behold, the angels of God were ascending and descending on it.

**13** And behold, the LORD stood above it and said, "I am the LORD, the God of Abraham your father and the God of Isaac. The land on which you lie I will give to you and to your offspring.

**14** Your offspring shall be like the dust of the earth, and you shall spread abroad to the west and to the east and to the north and to the south, and in you and in your offspring shall all the families of the earth be blessed.

**15** Behold, I am with you and will keep you wherever you go, and will bring you back to this land. For I will not leave you until I have done what I have promised you."

**16** Then Jacob awoke from his sleep and said, "Surely the LORD is in this place, and I did not know it."

**17** And he was afraid and said, "How awesome is this place! This is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven."

**18** So Jacob rose early in the morning and took the stone that he had put under his head and set it up as a pillar and poured oil on the top of it.

**19** He called the name of that place Bethel, though the name of the city was Luz at first.

**20** Then Jacob made a vow, saying, "If God will be with me and will keep me in this way that I go, and will give me bread to eat and clothing to wear,

**21** so that I come back to my father's house in peace, then the LORD shall be my God,

**22** and this stone, which I have set up as a pillar, shall be God's house. And of all that you give me I will give a full tenth to you."

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Source Manuscripts

This translation was produced from direct reading of four manuscript image sources:

1. **Leningrad Codex (Codex Leningradensis, B19A)**, dated c. 1008 CE. West Semitic Research Project photographs by Bruce E. Zuckerman. Genesis 28 spans Folios 15v–16r across two pages.

   **Folio 15v** (GEN_028_page_001.jpg): The verso of Folio 15, presented in inverted orientation per WSRP imaging convention. The bottom portion of this folio carries the close of Genesis 27 (Rebekah's complaint about the Hittite women) and transitions into Genesis 28. The parashah marker and section break between chapters are visible. The Tiberian vocalization and cantillation marks are intact throughout. This folio carries Genesis 28:1 through approximately 28:15 — Isaac's formal blessing and charge to Jacob, Esau's observation and corrective marriage to Mahalath, Jacob's departure from Beersheba, his arrival at the unnamed place, the dream of the stairway with ascending and descending angels, and the LORD's speech of covenant renewal.

   **Folio 16r** (GEN_028_page_002.jpg): The recto of Folio 16, in normal orientation with folio number "16" visible in the upper margin. This folio carries Genesis 28:16 through 28:22 and continues into the beginning of Genesis 29 — Jacob's awakening and recognition speech, his fear and naming of the place ("How awesome is this place!"), the setting up of the stone pillar, the naming of Bethel, and the conditional vow. The end of the chapter is clearly marked by a Masoretic parashah break before Genesis 29 begins.

2. **Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209)** — Septuagint/LXX, 4th century CE. Two pages were read directly.

   **Page 24** (GEN_028_vaticanus_page_001.jpg): Vaticanus page 24 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0024). The chapter marker "CAP. XXXI" is visible at the top of this page from Genesis 27 material. This page carries the end of Genesis 27 and the first portion of Genesis 28 in continuous uncial text across three columns. The Genesis 28 material visible here covers verses 1–15 approximately: Isaac's charge to Jacob, the El Shaddai blessing, the Abrahamic land grant, Jacob's departure, Esau's secondary marriage, and the ladder dream with the LORD's covenant speech. The uncial script is legible throughout with the LXX's characteristic continuous writing and minimal punctuation.

   **Page 25** (GEN_028_vaticanus_page_002.jpg): Vaticanus page 25 (digi.vatlib.it page pa_0025). This page carries the conclusion of Genesis 28 (vv. 16–22) and continues into Genesis 29. The material includes Jacob's awakening, the naming of Bethel, the pillar anointing, and the conditional vow. The three-column layout continues.

---

### Key Translation Decisions

**1. El Shaddai (v. 3) — "God Almighty"**

The divine name in verse 3 is אֵל שַׁדַּי (El Shaddai). This name appears here as Isaac's invocation when pronouncing the Abrahamic blessing upon Jacob — it is the same name by which God identified himself to Abraham (Genesis 17:1) and under which the patriarchal covenant of fruitfulness and land was given. The precise meaning of שַׁדַּי (Shaddai) is contested: older traditions associate it with the Akkadian šadu ("mountain") or with the Hebrew šad ("breast," connoting nurture and abundance); modern scholarship has not settled the question. The translation "God Almighty" follows the longstanding formal equivalence convention established from the LXX (θεὸς παντοκράτωρ — "God the Almighty/Omnipotent") and carried into the Latin Vulgate (Deus omnipotens) and thence into the English tradition. It is acknowledged that this rendering is interpretive — Shaddai likely does not derive from a root meaning "almighty" — but it is the most widely recognized English rendering of this specific divine name, and the formal translation preserves it as El Shaddai is a covenant-specific title for the God who acts in the patriarchal narratives. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders ὁ θεός μου ὁ παντοκράτωρ (ho theos mou ho pantokratōr — "my God the Almighty"), adding the possessive "my" which the MT lacks. The formal translation follows the MT: "God Almighty" without the possessive.

**2. סֻלָּם (sullam) — "stairway" (v. 12)**

The word סֻלָּם (sullam) appears only once in the entire Hebrew Bible — here in Genesis 28:12. This hapax legomenon has been traditionally rendered "ladder" (KJV, ASV) but the weight of modern scholarship favors "stairway" or "ramp." The root is likely connected to סָלַל (salal, "to heap up, to raise, to build a causeway") — the same root behind the word סְלִלָה (selilah, "a raised highway, a causeway"). In the ancient Near East, the most prominent architectural structure in which a stairway connected earth to heaven was the ziggurat — the Mesopotamian temple-tower with its external staircases. Jacob is in transit from Canaan toward Mesopotamia (Haran), and the vision of a earth-to-heaven structure with divine beings ascending and descending would have resonated with Mesopotamian temple imagery. The ascending-before-descending order of the angels — "ascending and descending" not "descending and ascending" — is often noted: the angels were already on earth (engaged in earthly tasks, perhaps guarding Jacob) and now ascend to report before descending again. The formal translation renders "stairway" as more consonant with the physical reality implied by the root and the architectural context, while acknowledging that "ladder" has deep tradition. The LXX renders κλίμαξ (klimax — "ladder, staircase, flight of steps") — a word that covers both runged ladders and stairways. The LXX does not resolve the ambiguity, but klimax in classical usage more often refers to a staircase or graduated structure than a handheld ladder.

**3. "The LORD stood above it" / עָלָיו ('alav) — ambiguity in v. 13**

Verse 13 opens with וְהִנֵּה יְהוָה נִצָּב עָלָיו (vehinneh Adonai nitsav alav — "and behold, the LORD was standing above it / upon him"). The pronoun עָלָיו (alav) is ambiguous: its antecedent is either the stairway (הַסֻּלָּם, hasullam — masculine noun) or Jacob himself (masculine). The two readings produce different visions: (a) "the LORD stood at the top of it [the stairway]" — God is visible at the apex of the divine stairway, the apex of the connection between heaven and earth; (b) "the LORD stood beside him [Jacob]" — God is positioned next to the sleeping Jacob, the vision being an intimate divine-human encounter at ground level. Most ancient interpreters and modern formal translations prefer (a) — God stands at the top of the stairway, marking himself as the one to whom the stairway leads. The formal translation renders "the LORD stood above it" (referring to the stairway), following the most natural reading of the syntax (the immediately preceding noun is the stairway). The LXX renders ἐπεστήρικτο δὲ ἐπ᾽ αὐτῆς (epestēriktō de ep' autēs — "and he was standing supported upon it"), using the feminine αὐτῆς — which, in Greek, could refer to the ladder (κλίμαξ is feminine in Greek) or to the earth (γῆ is feminine). The LXX appears to favor the stairway as antecedent, consistent with the formal translation's rendering.

**4. "All the families of the earth shall be blessed" (v. 14) — hitbarekhu**

The verbal form וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ (venivrekhu vekha — "and shall be blessed in you") uses the Niphal stem of בָּרַךְ (barakh, "to bless"). The Niphal can be either passive ("shall be blessed") or reflexive/middle ("shall bless themselves"). The same covenantal formula appears in Genesis 12:3 and 22:18, where the forms differ (Niphal vs. Hitpael), and the passive vs. reflexive interpretation has significant theological weight: does the formula promise that all nations will receive blessing through Abraham's/Jacob's offspring, or that all nations will invoke the patriarch's name as the paradigm of blessing ("may you be blessed as Abraham/Jacob is blessed")? Most modern formal translations, following the majority of ancient witnesses including the LXX, prefer the passive reading. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders ἐνευλογηθήσονται ἐν σοί (eneulogēthēsontai en soi — "shall be blessed in you"), clearly passive. The formal translation renders "shall be blessed," following the passive reading and the LXX.

**5. "A certain place" / הַמָּקוֹם (v. 11)**

The word הַמָּקוֹם (hammakom, "the place") is used repeatedly in this chapter (vv. 11 [twice], 16, 17, 19) and acquires increasing theological freight as the narrative progresses. At the beginning of verse 11, "the place" is just a place — a nameless stopping-point on the road to Haran. By verse 17, "this place" (הַמָּקוֹם הַזֶּה, hammakom hazeh) is "none other than the house of God." The word מָקוֹם (makom) also became a rabbinic name for God himself (HaMakom — "The Place") as a divine title expressing omnipresence. The formal translation renders verse 11 with the indefinite "a certain place" to convey the initial anonymity, then shifts to the more weighty "this place" in verse 17 as the text itself demands, preserving the narrative arc from anonymous stopping-point to sacred site.

**6. "Pillar" / מַצֵּבָה (massebah, v. 18)**

The מַצֵּבָה (massebah, "standing stone, pillar, memorial column") is a significant cultic object throughout the Hebrew Bible. Standing stones (massebot) could mark covenants (Genesis 31:45–52), theophanies (this passage), or graves (Genesis 35:20). Later legislation in Deuteronomy (7:5; 12:3) prohibits the destruction of Canaanite massebot but also discourages their use in Israelite worship — suggesting that the custom was widespread and contested. Jacob's massebah here is presented positively: he anoints it with oil and declares it God's house. The formal translation renders "pillar" (following the traditional English rendering) rather than "standing stone" or "stele," as "pillar" preserves the monumental, vertical sense of the object and has the longest English tradition. The LXX renders στήλην (stēlēn — "stele, pillar, monument"), consistent with the formal translation.

**7. "A full tenth" / עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ (v. 22) — the tithe vow**

Jacob's vow concludes with עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ לָךְ (asser a'asserennu lakh — "I will fully tithe it to you"). The form uses the cognate accusative construction — the infinitive absolute עַשֵּׂר (asser) with the finite verb אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ (a'asserennu) — conveying thoroughness and emphasis: "I will indeed tithe / I will tithe fully." This is the earliest explicit mention of the tithe in the canonical Genesis narrative (Abraham's tithe to Melchizedek in Genesis 14:20 is the only prior instance). The formal translation renders "I will give a full tenth to you," preserving the emphatic force of the cognate accusative without producing the awkward English "I will tithe-tithe it." The LXX renders ἀποδεκατώσω αὐτό σοι (apodekatōsō auto soi — "I will tithe it to you"), using a simple future without the emphatic repetition of the MT. The formal translation retains the emphatic "full tenth" to capture the MT's intensive construction.

**8. The Conditional Vow (vv. 20–22) — theological significance**

Jacob's vow is structured as a conditional: "if God will be with me... then the LORD shall be my God." This conditionality has generated considerable theological discussion. Is Jacob bargaining with God? Is this an immature faith that the narrative presents without endorsement? Or is the conditional structure a formal covenantal formula — not a doubt but a pledge? The formal translation preserves the conditional exactly as the text presents it ("If God will be with me... then the LORD shall be my God"), without smoothing it into an unconditional declaration. The Hebrew אִם (im, "if") is unambiguously conditional. Jacob is not the mature Israel of the later narratives; he is a fugitive who has just received a theophany and responds with what faith he has. The text records the vow honestly. The LXX (Vaticanus) renders ἐὰν ᾖ κύριος ὁ θεὸς μετ' ἐμοῦ (ean ē kyrios ho theos met' emou — "if the Lord God is with me"), also conditional, consistent with the formal translation.

**9. "Beersheba" and English Place-Name Forms**

Place names follow established English convention: Beersheba (בְּאֵר שָׁבַע), Haran (חָרָן), Bethel (בֵּית אֵל), Paddan-aram (פַּדַּן אֲרָם). The name Bethel is itself a translation — בֵּית אֵל (bet-El) means "house of God" — which is made explicit in the narrative at verse 17 ("the house of God") and in the naming etiology at verse 19. The formal translation preserves both the place name "Bethel" and the Hebrew meaning "house of God" in verse 17, as the text itself plays on both layers.

**10. "The LORD" for YHWH throughout**

The Tetragrammaton יְהוָה (YHWH) appears at verses 13, 16, and 21 (and implicitly in v. 15 "what I have promised you"). It is rendered "the LORD" throughout, consistent with the project's formal convention. At verse 3, the divine name used is אֵל שַׁדַּי (El Shaddai), rendered "God Almighty." At verse 13, when the LORD identifies himself, the construction is אֲנִי יְהוָה (ani Adonai — "I am the LORD") — the theophanic self-identification formula used at Sinai (Exodus 20:2) and throughout the patriarchal narratives. The formal translation preserves this formula exactly.

---

### LXX (Vaticanus) Variants — Genesis 28

Pages 24–25 of Codex Vaticanus were read directly. The continuous uncial Greek text was legible across both pages. Key variants observed:

| Verse | MT (Leningrad Codex) | LXX (Vaticanus) | Translation Decision | Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 28:3 | אֵל שַׁדַּי ("El Shaddai") | ὁ θεός μου ὁ παντοκράτωρ ("my God the Almighty") | "God Almighty" | LXX adds possessive "my"; MT lacks it; formal translation follows MT |
| 28:3 | וְיַפְרְךָ וְיַרְבֶּךָ ("and make you fruitful and multiply you") | αὐξάνοι σε καὶ πληθύνοι σε ("may he increase you and multiply you") | "make you fruitful and multiply you" | MT uses פָּרָה (parah, "to be fruitful") + רָבָה (ravah, "to multiply"); LXX uses αὐξάνω ("to increase, grow") + πληθύνω ("to multiply") — LXX substitutes a more general growth term; formal translation follows MT's פָּרָה |
| 28:9 | מַחֲלַת ("Mahalath") | Μαελεθ | "Mahalath" | Standard name transliteration; LXX form slightly different |
| 28:12 | סֻלָּם ("stairway/ladder") | κλίμαξ ("staircase/ladder") | "stairway" | LXX klimax is ambiguous between ladder and staircase; formal translation renders "stairway" based on Hebrew root and ANE context |
| 28:12 | עֹלִים וְיֹרְדִים ("ascending and descending") | ἀναβαίνοντες καὶ καταβαίνοντες ("ascending and descending") | "ascending and descending" | MT and LXX agree; ascending before descending — angels were already on earth |
| 28:13 | נִצָּב עָלָיו ("standing above it/him") | ἐπεστήρικτο ἐπ᾽ αὐτῆς ("standing supported upon it") | "stood above it" | LXX uses feminine pronoun (αὐτῆς), referring to the ladder (κλίμαξ, feminine in Greek); supports stairway as antecedent |
| 28:14 | וְנִבְרְכוּ בְךָ ("shall be blessed in you") | ἐνευλογηθήσονται ἐν σοί ("shall be blessed in you") | "shall be blessed" | MT Niphal (passive/reflexive); LXX clearly passive; formal translation follows passive reading |
| 28:15 | לֹא אֶעֱזָבְךָ ("I will not leave you") | οὐ μή σε ἐγκαταλίπω ("I will by no means abandon you") | "I will not leave you" | LXX uses double negative (οὐ μή) for strong negation; MT simple negation; both express the same covenant assurance |
| 28:17 | בֵּית אֱלֹהִים ("house of God") | οἶκος θεοῦ ("house of God") | "house of God" | MT and LXX agree exactly |
| 28:19 | לוּז ("Luz") | Οὐλαμμαυς ("Oulammaous") | "Luz" | LXX appears to have read or transliterated a different form — possibly conflating אוּלָם (ulam, "porch/hall") with the name; MT Luz is the established city name; formal translation follows MT |
| 28:20–21 | אִם...וְהָיָה ("if...then") | ἐὰν...ἔσται ("if...shall be") | "If...then" | MT and LXX both fully conditional; formal translation preserves the conditional |
| 28:22 | עַשֵּׂר אֲעַשְּׂרֶנּוּ ("I will fully tithe it") | ἀποδεκατώσω αὐτό ("I will tithe it") | "I will give a full tenth" | MT has emphatic cognate accusative; LXX simple future; formal translation captures MT's emphasis |

---

### Structural and Theological Notes

**Jacob's Departure and the Transition at Beersheba**

Genesis 28 opens where Genesis 27 closed: Rebekah has told Jacob to flee to Laban in Haran (27:43), and Isaac now formally sends him with a blessing that reiterates and transfers the Abrahamic covenant (vv. 3–4). The sending is public and official — Isaac invokes El Shaddai and explicitly grants Jacob "the blessing of Abraham." This formal transmission is the bridge between the stolen blessing of chapter 27 (which was primarily about material prosperity and political lordship) and the Abrahamic covenant blessing (which is about land, offspring, and being a blessing to the nations). The two blessings are now both in Jacob.

**Esau's Corrective Marriage (vv. 6–9)**

The brief Esau episode in verses 6–9 functions as ironic counterpoint. Esau observes that his Canaanite wives displeased Isaac and attempts to correct the situation by marrying Mahalath, daughter of Ishmael. The marriage is presented without authorial comment — the reader is left to assess whether Esau's corrective action is admirable (he tries to please his father) or futile (Ishmael is not the covenant line, so the choice, though not Canaanite, is still outside the narrowing of the covenant). The name Mahalath does not appear in the parallel list in Genesis 36:3, where the same wife appears to be called Basemath — a textual-critical discrepancy the formal translation does not resolve but notes here.

**The Stairway Dream — Theophany at the Nameless Place**

The theophany structure of Genesis 28:10–22 follows a classic biblical pattern: approach to an unnamed/sacred place → divine appearance → divine speech → human response → memorial action → naming. Jacob does not seek the theophany — he stops to sleep at a random location. The divine initiative is unambiguous. The stairway (sullam) is not Jacob's ladder to heaven; it is heaven's conduit to earth, pre-existing and already populated with ascending and descending angels. Jacob is shown — not climbed to — the divine presence. The covenant speech of the LORD (vv. 13–15) repeats the land-promise (v. 13), the offspring-promise (v. 14a), the international blessing (v. 14b), and adds a new element specific to Jacob's situation: personal divine accompaniment and safe return (v. 15). This last element responds directly to Jacob's circumstances as a fugitive.

**"The Gate of Heaven" and Ancient Near Eastern Background**

Jacob's exclamation in verse 17 — "this is none other than the house of God, and this is the gate of heaven" (שַׁעַר הַשָּׁמָיִם, sha'ar hashamayim) — reflects Mesopotamian cosmology in which certain sacred sites (particularly ziggurat temples) were understood as the points where heaven and earth met, where divine beings passed between realms. The Babylonian name for Babylon itself was bāb-ilim — "gate of god(s)" — cognate with the Hebrew בֵּית אֵל (bet-El, Bethel, "house of God"). The naming of Bethel is thus charged with competitive polemic: the true "house of God" and "gate of heaven" is not Babylon's ziggurat but this unassuming field in Canaan where the God of the covenant appeared to a sleeping fugitive. The formal translation preserves this naming dynamic without interpretive addition.

**The Conditional Vow and Jacob's Theological Development**

The conditional structure of Jacob's vow (vv. 20–22) — "if God will be with me... then the LORD shall be my God" — is the most theologically discussed element of the chapter. It stands in contrast to the covenant promises just given unconditionally by the LORD in verses 13–15. The LORD promised without condition; Jacob responds with condition. This asymmetry is characteristic of Jacob's character across the Genesis narrative: he is a bargainer, a calculator, a man who wants guarantees. The formal translation preserves this conditionality scrupulously. The vow is also Jacob's first personal, individual statement of faith — whatever its conditional structure, it is the moment when the covenant promise given to Abraham and Isaac first becomes Jacob's own. The final clause — "and of all that you give me I will give a full tenth to you" — grounds the covenantal relationship in a concrete, ongoing material act of loyalty.

---

— LOGOS
